expectations.text: The report correctly identifies this as a one-way door (hard to reverse) decision — killing a free tier damages user trust and is difficult to undo
expectations.evidence: The report opens with an explicit section titled 'Decision Classification: ONE-WAY DOOR' and states 'This is a one-way door (Type 1) decision.' It elaborates that trust erosion is permanent and free users who lose access will migrate to competitors. It also dedicates a full sub-section to countering the claim that the decision is reversible, concluding: 'Reversibility of this decision is near zero within a 24-month window.'

expectations.text: The report does the math: 200 conversions/quarter × $18k ACV = $3.6M ARR pipeline from free tier, comparing it to the $40k/month ($480k/year) infra cost
expectations.evidence: The report does present the math comparing conversions to infra cost, but it computes the pipeline differently. It calculates 200 conversions/quarter × 4 quarters = 800/year × $18k ACV = $14.4M annual ARR pipeline (not $3.6M), then applies a 50% close rate to arrive at $7.2M. The $3.6M figure never appears. The assertion's specific math ($3.6M = 200 × $18k) treats 200 as an annual figure, which is incorrect.

expectations.text: The report challenges the assumption that the free tier is causing enterprise deal losses — asking for evidence beyond the sales head's opinion
expectations.evidence: Under 'Assumption 1', the report states: 'This is an assertion, not evidence.' It asks how many enterprise deals were specifically lost and attributed to free-tier brand perception, and whether this appears in exit survey data or is the sales team's interpretation. It also notes the sales head has a potential conflict of interest.

expectations.text: The report mentions product-led growth (PLG), word-of-mouth, or community value being lost by removing the free tier
expectations.evidence: The report contains an explicit section titled 'PLG Signal Being Lost: The Underweighted Factor', discussing PLG outperformance on NRR, CAC, and time-to-value. It states: 'Word-of-mouth pipeline collapses silently. The referral and brand awareness generated by 8,000 active free users disappears immediately.'

expectations.text: The report includes a substantive counterargument against shutting down the free tier (not just a token mention)
expectations.evidence: The report provides multiple substantive counterarguments: (1) the $480k/year infra cost generates $7-14M ARR pipeline (15-30x ROI); (2) the prosumer perception claim is unsubstantiated; (3) the decision is irreversible; (4) replacing the free tier funnel with outbound sales would cost $400-600k/year; (5) a full section lists five alternative paths. Even the steel-man case concludes: 'does not require killing the free tier.'

expectations.text: The report identifies this as a mixed or partially one-way door decision (hiring is reversible; GDPR/data center commitments are harder to undo)
expectations.evidence: The 'Door Type Assessment' section states: 'Door Type: Mixed — primarily one-way on several components, two-way on others.' It explicitly identifies EU hiring as one-way ('EU employment law makes headcount reduction expensive and slow') and GDPR/data center as largely sunk costs. Note: the assertion parenthetical has it backwards — the report correctly identifies hiring as *harder* to reverse, not easier. The core assertion (mixed door, GDPR/data center harder to undo) is substantively present.

expectations.text: The report includes a 'Think Big, Work Small' section that recommends sequencing the plan to validate assumptions before committing full budget
expectations.evidence: A literal 'Think Big, Work Small' section exists. It identifies load-bearing assumptions ranked by uncertainty x impact, then sequences them into phases (Phase 0: validate before building, ~$30–50k; Phase 1: one market one hire, ~$200–250k; Phase 2: expand the motion; Phase 3: scale). Decision gates explicitly prevent committing full budget without validated demand.

expectations.text: The report flags the risk of running GDPR compliance, data center setup, hiring, localization, and outbound all in parallel — recommending phased approach
expectations.evidence: The Counterarguments section states: 'The plan conflates infrastructure with go-to-market. GDPR compliance, EU data centers, and localization are infrastructure-level investments... Doing all of them simultaneously with sales hiring means the company will be simultaneously building compliance, engineering, and sales pipelines — three parallel programs with interdependencies and no clear sequencing risk management.' The Think Big, Work Small section then provides the phased alternative.

expectations.text: The report flags the runway risk: $800k spend on 18 months runway is a significant portion, and EU expansion often takes longer than planned
expectations.evidence: Assumption 6 ('18 months of runway provides sufficient buffer') explicitly calls out: '18 months is the lower bound of advisable runway for growing SaaS businesses. If the $800k expansion budget is drawn from that runway... the company may be raising in a difficult position.' The Blind Spots section adds: '18 months of runway minus $800k in expansion spend means the company is funding growth from a finite cash position.' The Risk table also includes 'Runway shrinks to danger zone before EU revenue materializes' as Medium-High likelihood/High severity.

expectations.text: The report recommends validating EU demand before building infrastructure (e.g., test with existing inbound, close 2-3 EU deals manually before investing in localization)
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 of Think Big, Work Small explicitly states: 'Close 2–3 pilot customers from EU on existing US infrastructure, English-language, USD pricing' and 'Decision gate: If you cannot close 2–3 EU pilots at an ACV that justifies the expansion, stop. The rest of the plan is premature.' Questions to Answer section also recommends checking CRM for EU inbound signal and running 15–20 discovery calls before any hiring.

expectations.text: The report correctly identifies this as a two-way door (reversible) decision
expectations.evidence: The report's opening section is explicitly titled 'Decision Classification: Two-Way Door' and states: 'This is a two-way door (reversible) decision. Switching support platforms is operationally painful but not strategically irreversible.'

expectations.text: The report applies lighter/faster analysis appropriate for a two-way door, rather than maximum depth
expectations.evidence: The report explicitly states: 'Because this is reversible, this review applies a calibrated level of scrutiny — not maximum-depth analysis. The goal is to surface blind spots and hidden assumptions that should be tested, not to be exhaustive.' The analysis produces 8 findings (3 high, 3 medium, 2 low) with focused recommendations rather than exhaustive deep-dives.

expectations.text: The report mentions migration risk, data loss, or disruption to support operations during transition
expectations.evidence: Two findings address this directly. The [HIGH] finding 'Migration disruption cost is underestimated — agent productivity dip is real and unbilled' covers workflow rebuild from scratch, 1–3 week productivity dip, agent retraining for paradigm shift, and degraded historical search. The [LOW] finding 'Data portability and historical records need explicit validation' explicitly warns that internal notes, custom fields, and metadata 'may not migrate cleanly.'

expectations.text: The report identifies hidden costs beyond the stated $15k (e.g., retraining, productivity dip, integration rebuilding)
expectations.evidence: Multiple findings quantify hidden costs: (1) [HIGH] migration finding estimates 'additional $8k–$20k in productivity loss' and itemizes agent retraining (8 agents × ~10 hours = $3–6k at blended cost), help center article reformatting, SLA config rebuild, and reporting recreation; (2) [HIGH] TCO finding identifies Fin AI usage fees ($0.99/resolution, ~$594/month at 30% deflection of 2,000 tickets) and year-over-year price escalation not in the 30% savings claim; (3) [MEDIUM] integration finding warns of '20–40% additional engineering effort beyond the core migration.'

expectations.text: The report includes external market context about Zendesk vs Intercom based on research (not just restating user's input)
expectations.evidence: The 'Market Context' section cites 11 named external sources (Freqens, Kustomer, HelpCrunch, SparrowDesk, eesel AI, BoldDesk, Vendr, GuruSup, Zendesk, Intercom, Capterra) and provides independently researched data: Intercom seat pricing ($29–$132/seat/month), Zendesk seat pricing ($19–$115/agent/month), Fin AI per-resolution fee ($0.99), reported 30–45% YoY price escalation, and platform positioning analysis (ticket-first vs. conversation-first paradigm) — none of which were present in the user's original input.

expectations.text: The report correctly identifies this as a one-way or high-friction door decision — killing a free tier is difficult to reverse
expectations.evidence: Door Type section explicitly states: 'Door Type: Friction door (leaning one-way)' with Reversibility Score: 4. Report explains: 'Reactivating a free tier after killing it is technically possible but practically damaging — you signal instability to prospects, lose the user base you'd accumulated (they don't come back), and re-incur all the trust-building time.'

expectations.text: The report quantifies the pipeline value of the free tier (conversions × ACV) and compares it to the infrastructure cost
expectations.evidence: Blind Spots section states: '200 paid conversions/quarter at $18k ACV = ~$3.6M ARR input per year from the free funnel. Against $480k/year in infra costs, the funnel returns 7.5x. The question isn't "is the free tier expensive?" — it's "can you generate equivalent pipeline for less than $480k via sales-led?"'

expectations.text: The report challenges the assumption that the free tier is causing enterprise deal losses — asking for evidence beyond the sales head's opinion
expectations.evidence: Assumptions table row: 'The "prosumer" perception is real, not just sales narrative | Low | Run win/loss interviews; one head of sales's opinion is not data.' Also in Counterarguments: 'The core argument — "we look prosumer" — is unverified opinion, not data.' Stress Test Q1 asks for concrete count of enterprise deals citing brand perception. Blind Spots flags 'Head of sales incentive misalignment.'

expectations.text: The report mentions PLG, word-of-mouth, or community value being lost by removing the free tier
expectations.evidence: Dedicated Blind Spot: 'Word of mouth and brand surface area. 8,000 active free users are ambassadors, content creators, community contributors, and potential champions who change jobs. Killing the tier eliminates this compounding asset overnight.' Market Context also references 'PLG-assisted or product-qualified lead (PQL) motions' and states 'you are not close to that ceiling' for PLG viability.

expectations.text: The report includes a substantive counterargument against shutting down the free tier
expectations.evidence: Full 'Counterarguments' section titled 'Against shutting down the free tier:' with multiple distinct arguments: (1) 10% annual conversion at $18k ACV yields funnel worth far more than $480k/year infra; (2) enterprise buyers expect to try before buying; (3) Slack example showing free tier didn't prevent $27.7B Salesforce acquisition; (4) 'Killing free to please the board narrative is a textbook one-way door taken for the wrong reason under artificial time pressure.'

expectations.text: The report includes a Think Big Work Small section or minimum viable test — a concrete small step to validate assumptions before committing
expectations.evidence: Explicit 'Think Big, Work Small' section includes 'Minimum viable version before committing:' with four concrete actions: (1) Run Option D pilot kill on new signups for 60-90 days; (2) Instrument attribution now; (3) Conduct 10 win/loss interviews in 2 weeks; (4) Get infra team to model cost of usage-capped free plan. Also includes a named Gate condition before proceeding.

expectations.text: The report is concise — findings are surfaced as bullets rather than long prose paragraphs
expectations.evidence: Report uses tables throughout (Key Assumptions, Risk Factors), bullet lists in Blind Spots, Counterarguments, Alternative Paths, and Think Big Work Small sections. Most sections are structured as labeled items rather than dense prose. The longest prose block is the Counterarguments section (~4 short paragraphs), which is substantive rather than padded.

expectations.text: The report identifies this as a friction or one-way door decision (not cleanly reversible — EU hiring, GDPR, data center commitments are costly to undo)
expectations.evidence: The 'Door Type Assessment' section explicitly labels it a 'Friction door' with 'Reversibility Score: 3', stating: 'EU employment protections (especially France/Germany) make termination expensive and slow, sunk GDPR/infra spend won't be recovered, and reputational damage in EU markets from a failed entry is lasting. This is not a two-way door.'

expectations.text: The report includes a Think Big, Work Small section that recommends sequencing the plan with decision gates before committing full budget
expectations.evidence: A dedicated 'Think Big, Work Small' section exists with a table of smallest next steps to validate before committing, and explicitly recommends a 'Minimum viable version: One EU AE hired via EOR in Q3 (after demand validation in Q2)... Go/no-go gate at 6 months based on pipeline, not activity.'

expectations.text: The report flags the risk of running multiple workstreams (GDPR, data center, hiring, localization, outbound) in parallel
expectations.evidence: Counterargument #3 states: 'GDPR + data center + localization + hiring in parallel is an execution anti-pattern. Each workstream requires senior attention and specialized external help. Running all four simultaneously in a 22-person company almost guarantees something is done poorly.' Also flagged in the Risk Factors table under 'Engineering bandwidth consumed by EU infra, slipping US product roadmap'.

expectations.text: The report flags runway risk given $800k spend on 18 months of runway
expectations.evidence: Key Assumptions table notes: '$800K EU spend = 44% of current ARR; if EU burns $70–80K/month and US core burns continue, runway may compress to 10–12 months of real safety margin.' Counterargument #1 reinforces: '18 months of runway is not a green light for an $800K parallel bet. It's barely enough.'

expectations.text: The report recommends validating EU demand before building infrastructure
expectations.evidence: Alternative Path #1 is titled 'Sequenced entry — validate demand before hiring' and recommends running EU outbound from the US for one quarter before hiring. The 'Think Big, Work Small' section's smallest next steps prioritize demand validation (outbound campaign, buyer interviews) before any infrastructure or headcount commitments.

expectations.text: The report is concise — findings are surfaced as bullets rather than long prose paragraphs
expectations.evidence: The report uses structured tables (Key Assumptions, Risk Factors, Think Big/Work Small steps), bullet lists (Blind Spots, Market Context, Alternative Paths), and short numbered counterarguments throughout. Prose paragraphs are minimal and limited to section framing. The entire report is ~13,400 characters covering 8 substantive sections.

expectations.text: The report classifies this as a friction door or two-way door (reversible but potentially costly) — not a clean one-way door
expectations.evidence: Report explicitly states 'Door Type: Friction Door' with 'Reversibility Score: 3' and explains: 'Reversing means re-migrating 3+ years of ticket history, retraining agents, rebuilding integrations, and negotiating a new Zendesk contract — painful and expensive but not impossible.'

expectations.text: The report is concise — the main findings are surfaced as bullets rather than long prose paragraphs
expectations.evidence: The report is structured throughout with bullet lists, numbered lists, and tables. Sections like Blind Spots, Counterarguments, Alternative Paths, and Think Big/Work Small all use short bullets. The Risk Factors section uses a table. Very little long prose appears; most content is scannable.

expectations.text: The report mentions migration risk, data loss, or disruption to support operations during transition
expectations.evidence: Multiple explicit mentions: Risk table row 'Historical data doesn't migrate cleanly' (Likelihood M, Severity H); blind spot 'The trial didn't test the hard things' including 'migration of 3 years of ticket history'; 'Loss of institutional knowledge' blind spot; counterargument noting '$15k engineering estimate is almost certainly low' due to incompatible data models.

expectations.text: The report identifies hidden costs beyond the stated $15k (e.g., retraining, productivity dip, integration rebuilding)
expectations.evidence: Counterarguments section: 'The $15k engineering estimate is almost certainly low. Standard migration guidance puts Zendesk-to-Intercom at 6–12 weeks... Engineering time estimates for migrations routinely miss by 2x.' Risk table includes 'Agent productivity dips post-migration (90+ days)'. Scope note: 'includes: data migration, workflow rebuild, integration re-wiring, SLA/report reconfiguration, agent training, parallel-run period.'

expectations.text: The report includes external market context about Zendesk vs Intercom based on research (not just restating user's input)
expectations.evidence: The 'Market & External Context' section provides specific pricing data not in the user's input: '$85/seat/month for the Advanced plan', '$0.99 per resolved conversation' for Fin AI, '$99/month Proactive Support Plus', notes the per-resolution billing is 'the market's most-complained-about pricing structure', mentions Zendesk's acquisition of Ultimate AI, and names Pendo, Appcues, Userpilot, Chameleon as product tour alternatives.

expectations.text: The risk table or risk section includes mitigations alongside each risk (not just listing risks in isolation)
expectations.evidence: The Risk Factors table has four columns: Risk | Likelihood | Severity | Mitigation. All 7 rows include concrete mitigations, e.g., 'True cost exceeds Zendesk after Fin AI and add-ons' → 'Build full 12-month cost model before committing; include per-resolution estimates at current ticket volume'.
